title: Famous Cats in History: Newton, Churchill, Hemingway, and More 🐱
description: Discover the most influential cats in history. Isaac Newton and his invisible cat. Winston Churchill and his cats named after his enemies. How did cats influence great minds?

segments.voice: Throughout history, cats have been companions to some of the brightest minds in humanity. From scientists to politicians, cats have left their mark on world history.
Isaac Newton, one of the greatest scientists of all time, had a favorite cat. Newton was so close to his cat that he frequently experimented with it.
Newton supposedly cut a hole in the door of his house so his cat could enter and exit freely. A student then cut a larger hole for the cat's kitten, not understanding it wasn't necessary.
Although the story of the hole in the door is probably apocryphal, it's revealing how Newton's contemporaries associated his name with cat innovation. His attachment to his cat was notorious.
Winston Churchill, Prime Minister of Britain during World War II, was a confessed cat lover. He had several cats living in 10 Downing Street.
Churchill had an orange cat named Marmalade. There's an anecdote where Churchill was in an important war meeting and saw his cat was hungry. Churchill paused the meeting to feed the cat.
Churchill named some of his cats after his political enemies. He had cats named Hitler, Mussolini, and other dictators, as a sarcastic way to show his disdain.
Ernest Hemingway, the famous writer, had an extreme obsession with cats. As we mentioned, he had around 54 cats in his Key West house, many of them polydactyl.
Hemingway frequently wrote about his cats. Some of his friends reported that he spent so much time with his cats that he wrote less. The cat and writing were always competing for his attention.
Hemingway's most famous cat was Fuzzy, who supposedly sat on his desk while he wrote. Some speculate that Fuzzy inspired characters in his stories.
T.S. Eliot, the famous modernist poet, was a passionate cat lover. His collection of poems 'Old Possum's Book of Practical Cats' was directly inspired by his own feline cats.
Eliot wrote that cats were his best literary critics. He said that if a cat didn't pay attention to something he wrote, it probably wasn't worth writing.
Frida Kahlo, the Mexican artist, had numerous cats in her Mexico house. Her cats appear in several of her self-portraits, integrated as symbols of her artistic identity.
Frida's Blue House in Coyoacán was a sanctuary for animals, including cats. It's estimated that she had up to 50 cats living there at various times.
Florence Nightingale, the famous nurse, had a favorite cat named Bismarck. She took him with her on her nursing campaigns, and it's rumored that Bismarck had special access to Nightingale's heart.
Cardinal Richelieu, the powerful 17th-century French minister, had over 14 cats. It's known that he frequently had cats sitting with him during important political meetings.
It's reported that Richelieu even had a special bodyguard for his cats. The cats were so valuable to him that they received dedicated protection.

In conclusion, cats have been companions to some of the most influential individuals in history. This suggests that perhaps there's something special about cats that attracts creative and powerful minds.
